meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Coefficient of Friction Between Body and Wedge

intro.kicker: Topic 15 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Coefficient of Friction Between Body and Wedge
intro.description: When a body descends on a smooth wedge in time t and on the rough version of the same wedge in time nt (n > 1), the ratio of times encodes the coefficient of kinetic friction. Equating the path expressions for both cases gives the elegant result μ = tanθ [1 − 1/n²]. This formula directly connects the geometric property of the incline (θ) with the timing ratio (n) measured experimentally. NEET tests this as a numerical: given θ and n, find μ. The derivation uses kinematics on rough vs smooth wedge and is a single-concept, high-mark-density topic.

expectedQuestions.description: Appears as a direct numerical in NEET: given the angle θ of the wedge and the time ratio n (time on rough / time on smooth), compute μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²]. The derivation itself may also be tested as a fill-in MCQ. Seen in NEET previous papers and DC Pandey-level practice sets.

timeRequired.description: Lean, single-formula topic. 15 min to derive μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] from kinematics, 15 min on two to three NEET-pattern numericals. Know the standard result and be able to substitute θ and n quickly under exam pressure.

difficulty.description: The derivation is clean but the key trap is forgetting that n is the time ratio (rough to smooth), not a speed ratio. Students who confuse n with velocity ratio get μ = tanθ[1 − n²] (wrong sign in bracket). Also, the formula assumes the body starts from rest (u = 0) and travels the same path length on both wedges. If the problem changes either assumption the formula breaks. Always re-establish conditions before applying.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics does not cover the smooth-vs-rough wedge timing derivation. The formula μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] is specific to the Indian competitive exam syllabus at this depth. NRI students transitioning from AP or IB curricula will need to derive this from first principles, which can be done in 15 minutes but requires practising the n-ratio substitution until it is automatic.

weightage.sectionTitle: Coefficient of Friction Between Body and Wedge — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] is the single formula; tested every alternate year in NEET.
Questions supply numerical values of θ and n; expected output is μ.
Also tested conceptually: does μ depend on mass? (No.) Does it depend on wedge height? (No.)

strategySteps.title: Derive the Formula from Kinematics
strategySteps.description: For smooth wedge: S = ½ g sinθ · t². For rough wedge: S = ½ g(sinθ − μ cosθ)(nt)². Set equal since path S is the same, divide sides, isolate μ. This 4-step derivation must be written from memory under exam conditions.

strategySteps.title: Memorise the Final Expression
strategySteps.description: μ = tanθ [1 − 1/n²]. Note: when n = 1 (same time, impossible for a rough incline from rest), μ = 0 — a logical consistency check. When n → ∞ (body barely moves on rough wedge), μ → tanθ = the angle-of-repose condition.

strategySteps.title: Spot the Common Mistake on NEET
strategySteps.description: NEET often sets n = √2 or n = 2. For n = √2: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/2] = tanθ/2. For n = 2: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/4] = 3tanθ/4. Practice these specific substitutions to avoid arithmetic errors under pressure.

cards.title: Wedge Friction Formula Sheet
cards.description: Full derivation of μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] with annotated FBD for smooth and rough wedge, comparison table for different n values, and 5 solved NEET-pattern numericals.

cards.title: Wedge Friction Practice MCQs
cards.description: 10 MCQs covering: find μ given θ and n; find n given μ and θ; conceptual questions on mass-independence of μ; limiting wedge angle for n → ∞.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Coefficient of Friction Between Body and Wedge

cards.title: Wedge Friction Derivation
cards.coreRevision: A body starts from rest (u = 0) and slides down a wedge of angle θ. On smooth wedge, acceleration a₁ = g sinθ. On rough wedge, acceleration a₂ = g(sinθ − μ cosθ). Both cover the same path S; smooth takes time t, rough takes time nt. Apply S = ½a·t²: ½ g sinθ · t² = ½ g(sinθ − μ cosθ)(nt)². Dividing: sinθ = n²(sinθ − μ cosθ). Rearranging: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²].
points: Condition: both start from rest, same path length
At n → ∞: μ → tanθ (angle of repose condition)

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics and US curricula compare to NEET on the wedge-timing method for finding μ.

gaps.title: Timing Method for μ Not in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics would solve for μ using force balance or energy methods, never via the time-ratio formula. The specific approach of comparing descent times on smooth vs. rough incline and deriving μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] is exclusive to the Indian competitive exam preparation syllabus at this depth.
bullets: Practise deriving the formula end-to-end in under 2 minutes.
Know limiting cases: n = 1 (μ = 0, contradictory for rough) and n → ∞ (μ = tanθ).

questions.question: A body slides down a smooth wedge of angle 45° in time t. The same body slides down the rough version of the same wedge in time 2t. The coefficient of kinetic friction is:
questions.explanation: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] = tan45°[1 − 1/4] = 1 × 3/4 = 0.75. n = 2 (rough time is 2 × smooth time). tan45° = 1. So μ = 3/4.

questions.question: On a smooth wedge of inclination θ, a block descends in time t. On a rough wedge of the same angle, it takes nt seconds (n > 1). The coefficient of friction between block and rough wedge equals:
questions.explanation: From kinematics: equate S = ½g sinθ t² = ½g(sinθ − μ cosθ)(nt)². Divide: sinθ = n²(sinθ − μ cosθ). Rearrange: μ cosθ = sinθ(1 − 1/n²). So μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²]. Option D is wrong because (1 − n²) < 0 for n > 1.

questions.question: A block slides down a smooth incline (30°) in 2 s. On the rough version it takes 4 s. Find μ (g = 10 m/s²):
If θ = 45° and μ = 0.5, the ratio n (rough time / smooth time) is:
For μ = tanθ on a rough wedge, what does n equal?
The formula μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²] assumes which condition?
options: Body starts with initial velocity u
Body starts from rest and paths are equal
questions.answer: Body starts from rest and paths are equal

items.title: Write the formula and its conditions
items.description: μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²]. Conditions: u = 0 (body starts from rest), same path S for both wedges, n = rough time / smooth time > 1.

items.title: Verify limiting cases
items.description: n = 1 ⇒ μ = 0 (smooth, as expected). n → ∞ ⇒ μ → tanθ (angle of repose — body just barely slides on rough wedge).

items.title: Confirm mass-independence
items.description: The formula has no mass in it. μ between block and wedge is independent of the mass of the block. A common NEET MCQ tests this conceptual understanding directly.

items.question: Why does the mass of the block not appear in the formula?
items.answer: In the kinematics equations, the mass m cancels from both sides when equating S on smooth and rough wedge paths. This is because both mg sinθ (driving force) and μmg cosθ (friction force) are proportional to m. The acceleration is mass-independent, and the path comparison removes m entirely.

items.question: What does it mean physically when n → ∞?
items.answer: As n → ∞, the time on the rough wedge → ∞, meaning the block does not slide at all. This corresponds to μ → tanθ, which is exactly the condition for the angle of repose. The body is on the verge of sliding (static equilibrium) when μ = tanθ.

items.question: Is this formula valid if the block does not start from rest?
items.answer: No. The derivation uses S = ½at² which assumes u = 0. If the block has an initial velocity u, the path equation becomes S = ut + ½at², and the time ratio n no longer simplifies to the clean formula. This is an important exam trap to watch for in problem setups.

grounding_summary: Page 243 of the textbook presents the Coefficient of Friction Between a Body and Wedge derivation. A body slides on a smooth wedge in time t and on the rough wedge in time nt. Equating path lengths via kinematics gives sinθ = n²(sinθ − μ cosθ), leading to μ = tanθ[1 − 1/n²]. The result is independent of mass and depends only on the wedge angle and the time ratio.

The page explicitly states the derivation steps, uses S = ½(a)(t²) in both cases (smooth and rough), and arrives at the formula in factored tanθ form, confirming that the limiting case n → ∞ corresponds to μ = tanθ (angle-of-repose condition).
grounding_excerpt: The length of path in both the cases are same.
If the same wedge is made rough then time taken by it to come down becomes
A body slides on a smooth wedge of angle

review.notes: Content grounded on page 243 OCR. Formula mu = tan(theta)[1 - 1/n^2] derived and verified. Limiting cases (n=inf, n=1) confirmed. Mass-independence noted. Scope within chapter 05 friction.
